speaker: interviewee
text: Shh. [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before about when you arrived at the convent, I'd like you to describe in more detail your time there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the convent, as I said, food was rather scarce. A number of the girls had TB. When I was there two of them died of TB. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life was weird. We had to get up at 5:30 AM, had a quick wash. There were some small bottles of water on the bench. We couldn't undress for washing. Get dressed, make up the beds, tidy the bedrooms, go for mass. And later we had breakfast. We occasionally were getting a piece of bread, but mostly only barley. Then after breakfast we did various house duties. We were cleaning the rooms, painting the walls, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] washing the sheets in huge buckets of water, all done by hand. We were working in the kitchen, peeling whatever there was to be peeled, or washing the dishes, or helping cooking. Basically, almost all of the work was done by the girls. We had more prayers during the day, before meals, after meals. We had some sort of schooling. There was no general education whatsoever, but you had classes of sewing, knitting, and crocheting. And we were always in our spare time requested to knit or crochet things for the poor. The poor. [AUDIO OUT] We had once a month a bath, [AUDIO OUT] and [AUDIO OUT] --thought when they had their period. [AUDIO OUT] We were using rags or just the skirt that you were wearing to-- [AUDIO OUT] it was really quite miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to think. 20, 30 something-- [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the age range of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were young to mid-teens. I'd say about between 10 and 16, 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any other children there that were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, there. Not at all. I never knew, also, whether the nuns knew my identity or not. The pretense that my mother was my godmother was kept at all times. She came, I think, as I said, to visit me once or twice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The nuns were quite friendly to me. I think they considered me a good girl. I was trying to be unobtrusive and always obedient. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They mentioned, a number of times, that if I ever feel the vocation, I'd be welcomed there with open arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you adapt to the religious life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't thinking about it. I was just following the words and the gestures that were being done by everybody else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was also told time and again the story of Jews making matzos out of Christian babies' blood. They obviously sincerely believed that. They were not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were simply ignorant. Absolutely ignorant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you when you heard them saying things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just dismissed it. I had to keep living. I just discarded all that was unnecessary. I spent there about a year. One day, my mother came out of the blue. She'd learned that the woman who had put me in the convent was arrested and executed for sheltering Jews. And mother was frightened that she could have given someone my name. So she took me back to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, how long had you been in the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did not worry about the Germans. The thought was really far away. The war was something going on on faraway fronts. The German army was hardly seen in the convent or outside the convent. Or wherever, if ever we were in the street. I really did not worry about my safety while I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Siostry ?] [? Oblatki. ?] That was the name of the order of the sisters. They did not wear nuns' habits. They wore civilian clothes, but dark only. Black or dark brown. My mother took me back to Warsaw. She had been in Warsaw for some time, working as a servant in a Polish house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then she went to leave with her friend from her school years. I would like to say a few words about this lady. Her name was Aldona. She was a mixed-blood Polish and Georgian lady who had married a Jew, Polish Jew. Her maiden name was Jastrzebska, which is very Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-S-T-R-Z-E-B-S-K-A. Her husband's name was Lipschitz. Aldona was very dark, black hair, black eyes. Half Georgian. Dark skin. Her husband was Jewish. They had seven children who were all very dark-looking, very Jewish. Her husband died in 1938, and when the war broke out, Aldona had reverted to her maiden name. She kept all the children with her. They had a little house with a garden, sort of a single home with a garden, on the outskirts of Warsaw. And she stayed there with the children. Two of the oldest ones went to live in the country where they had a little piece of land with a house. And the rest was there in a part of Warsaw called Bielany. And in 19-- sometime in late '43 or early '44, my mother went to live there with her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know then-- I didn't realize until later after the war that Aldona had sheltered many Jews, that she had helped many people there in very hard times. She'd taken children who worked out of the ghetto and kept them until some safe place was found for them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I stayed in her place later. I'll come back to it. But my mother was there throughout '44. I was taken to Warsaw and placed in a sort of a girls' home, privately run. I don't know who paid for my stay there. I have no idea. I guess that Mother must have been getting some funds distributed in occupied Poland. There was money coming from London. And because she had those friends in the underground, in the socialist underground, she would have been getting some money through them. I lived in this girls' home. There was something like a dozen of us there. It was managed by two spinsters, middle-aged spinsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we all went to school, to different schools. Of course, all those schools were clandestine. No high schools at all were allowed in Poland. The school I'd gone to, [INAUDIBLE], was the same one my mother went as a girl, and my aunt went as a girl, and Aldona went to as a girl. It was a school run by two ladies, very good friends. It was called [INAUDIBLE]. The name of the school was [INAUDIBLE] It was run on very high ideals of enlightenment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I went to it, the classes were held. There was, again, maybe half a dozen girls per class, no more, run in private flats. The teachers there, some of them were my mother's teacher-- elderly by then. Others were her schoolmates. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the old times, the school had a custom that a lady called the class lady would sit at all the classes, supervising the girls, making sure that they behaved like ladies. During the occupation in this highly illegal school, we also had a little class lady, sitting there supervising us. She was then 82 years old. And I really think it quite incredible that an old woman like that, instead of living in peace, would risk her life to supervise a small group of girls who were also risking their life merely by going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We knew very few names. Anyway, names were not discussed. Best forgotten. It was a dangerous thing to know, names. To know too much. Anyway, I stayed in this girls' home till the end of July, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends with any of the other girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made friends. I made friends with a girl who was a year or two younger than I. Her name was Janka Nowicka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a shock to learn, when I met her after the war, not only that she was Jewish, but that she was my second cousin. Somehow or else her mother found out that she had been my father's cousin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Apparently there was at least another one who was Jewish. I don't know about the rest of the girls. But we were in hiding. We were in hiding everywhere. At this time, I also had to spend some days in the week in the so-called legal, open school. Which meant we had to work in the German workshop making uniforms for the army. That was the extent of schooling they were allowing. Uniforms or shoes. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] At this time, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we knew that the Russian army was advancing, that we'd soon become liberated. But that did not diminish the terror that was still rampant there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There was more and more talk of a general uprising being prepared in Warsaw. And I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with a group of girls. I'd gone to first aid training. We were to be employed as first aid, sort of, nursing assistants. In case of need to treat the wounded. We were also employed as message runners, the girls in the town. I was always very frightened to be carrying messages with German soldiers all over the place. But I guess I was just stupid. There was no real danger involved in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of messages would you be carrying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, various messages to various army-- underground army men. One that I remember is the hour and date of the uprising to begin. I had carried this to a couple of places. I stuck it in my sock. I don't know whether it was a very clever place. But that's where I carried it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to have contact with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember. Through other girls I knew. I really don't remember. I would like to tell you one more story from those times. My aunt Anna was employed as a nursing assistant to an old Polish lady. Her name was Stefania Sempolowska. She used to be a very well-known activist of the Polish Socialist Party. But that year, '43, '44, she was old. She never got out of bed. Was quite ill. In April 1943, 19th April, the ghetto uprising began. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Anna was telling us the story after the war. It was incredible. How did she know what was happening? There were no newspapers, there were no radios. There was no news of any sort getting through to her. She was in bed, she was never getting up. She was living on the ground floor of a little house, and upstairs lived her niece, who was entertaining German officers, as a rule. And this evening, while there was a party going on upstairs, she got out of bed, grabbed a broomstick, and started banging madly into the ceiling. 'The Jews are burning, and you are dancing there'. How did she know? It was quite incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my mother was living there at Aldona's in [? Wilanow, ?] which is far out of town. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had to take my post in a very different part of town, the other direction. On the first of August, the Warsaw Uprising started. And the part of town where I was in on the first day was overrun by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were all herded onto a big, empty block of land where there used to-- called [INAUDIBLE] where the peasants used to bring vegetable crops to the city. Sort of a wholesale market. And there were thousands of people there, young and old. And a lot of them wounded, and a lot of them sick. Thousands upon thousands. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole thing was run by a group of Ukrainians who were in the German army. They were absolutely terrifying. They were running around screaming and shooting into people helter-skelter, just without even looking. Then a group of SS men arrived and relief, they brought some of that. So sometimes the worst here is not the worst here. Anyway, I was with a group of nurses and para-nurses and first aid girls. And when the SS men arrived, our leader, who was an older nurse, asked them whether we could walk out with a Red Cross flag. And they allowed us to walk out. So we went out on the road out of town. I wanted to go where my mother was, which was something like going from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Williamstown to Dandenong. Maybe not quite so far away, but we had to go completely out of the suburbs into the forest, and across side roads and forests through to the other place. I had gone all by myself, because all the other girls went their way. I was very frightened. There were German posts everywhere. There was fighting in Warsaw, and they were all sort of-- there was the army on all the roads. But they never stopped me. I walked and walked for God knows how many hours until I arrived in [? Wilanow ?] where my mother was. And it turned out that she wasn't there. That when the uprising started, she'd walked into the center of town, where I lived in the girls' home, to look for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed at Aldona's. I stayed there. I worked in a little local field hospital that was mostly looking after a bunch of sick, elderly ladies. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That area then was controlled by Vlasovs, members of the Vlasov army. They were absolute savages.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember an episode when they chased us through the gardens there, screaming [POLISH], which means kill, kill. But somehow we managed to get out of it, and I stayed there until the end of the Warsaw Uprising, which was end of September. That's almost two months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the Germans emptied Warsaw of all population. It's become a desert city. So what happened to us is that they packed the few ill ladies that we were looking after into an army, sort of mock ambulance. Just a converted truck. And a couple girls who were there. And took us to a transit camp in Prushkov that all the people from Warsaw were taken to. And from there, they were sent to labor camps in Germany as slave labor, mostly. Or to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The driver of our truck started talking to us on the way. He was Austrian, an Austrian peasant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were three girls in the truck. And when we went far out of town, somewhere in the fields in the middle of nowhere, he stopped and told us, now you scram. Just run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ran away, but where was I to go? Did the three girls--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was I to go? I was in the fields. I went into-- there was a sort of string of little townships outside of Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew no one there. I didn't know what to do with myself. I had no money. So I went back to Czestochowa to my nuns. And they took me with open arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A train. I met there a woman who had been our sort of organizers in the so-called [? Darden ?] underground. And she gave me some dollars. She was, again, distributing the American dollars to whoever she knew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went back to the nuns. I was more than surprised when I walked in and the lady sitting there behind the door, like a sort of caretaker, was my mother's friend, who must have heard from my mother about this convent. And after the Warsaw Uprising, went there with her daughter. Anyway, the nuns took me with open arms, and I stayed with them until liberation. That was about 19th of January. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And when liberation came, I wanted to go Warsaw, back to Warsaw to look for my mother. The first train that went from Czestochowa around the 30th January, I got on. You didn't have to pay your fares then. You just, you know, sat. There were no seats. The seats were pulled out. There were just the frame and the holes in it. We went halfway to Warsaw. At a distance of about 70 kilometers, the train stopped. The tracks were ripped up further, so we couldn't go any further and just walked from there. I had a pair of old shoes that just about fell apart on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I just walked by myself. I stopped on the way where I knew some people, and they gave me a pair of galoshes, gumboots, that I wore instead of the shoes then. And I walked on by myself further on. I arrived in Warsaw that was still an almost empty town. There were some people starting to come back. It was mostly ruined. I went to the house in [? Mokotowska ?] Three, where we had lived before the war and the first year of the occupation. The house, amazing, it was standing. It had been occupied by the German army, so it wasn't destroyed. The caretaker was there, nobody else. He had never heard of my mother. He knew nothing. People were walking through town and leaving signs on walls, on destroyed buildings, on broken walls. They'd write in charcoal or in chalk, or in paint. So and so was here. And looking for so and so, leaving notes for each other. But there was no sign of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave a message on the wall yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left a message with the caretaker. He was there. He told me, you can stay here. Your flat's empty. But how was I to stay all by myself in an empty city?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- '45, I was 17, 18. No, sorry, '26. I was-- yes, I was 18. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to Czestochowa. On the way back, there was-- How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The track had already been repaired. And I went on an open coal track. Like, you know, those open trains, good trains that carry coal. I remember, it was in the middle of winter. It cost me two [INAUDIBLE] in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you were wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wearing a coat made out of an old Army blanket. And those gum galoshes. What I had underneath, I don't remember. But it wasn't very much for Polish February. I arrived in Czestochowa and stayed there for a while. And then I got news that my aunt Anna was back in her apartment in Lodz. Lodz hadn't been ruined. She had a whole big apartment that had been occupied by a German army officer during the war that she could go back to. So I went to Lodz and I stayed with her. There was still no word from my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to school. That was my last year of high school by then. February. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how exacting was our school curriculum, but by the end of June, we'd done-- actually, we'd done the high school certificate. For better or for worse. It was something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war finished on the 9th of May. There was still no sign from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can stop here for a tape break, all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
